name: piscola

metadata.name: piscola
metadata.summary: Type Ia Supernova Light-curve fitting code

documentation:
# PISCOLA

**Supernova light-curve fitting code in python**

Although the main purpose of PISCOLA is to fit type Ia supernovae, it can be used to fit other types of supernovae or even other transients.

[![repo](https://img.shields.io/badge/GitHub-temuller%2Fpiscola-blue.svg?style=flat)](https://github.com/temuller/piscola)
[![documentation status](https://readthedocs.org/projects/piscola/badge/?version=latest&style=flat)](https://piscola.readthedocs.io/en/latest/?badge=latest)
[![license](http://img.shields.io/badge/license-MIT-blue.svg?style=flat)](https://github.com/temuller/piscola/blob/master/LICENSE)
[![Build and Tests](https://github.com/temuller/piscola/actions/workflows/main.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/temuller/piscola/actions/workflows/main.yml)
[![Coverage](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/temuller/piscola/master/coverage.svg)](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/temuller/piscola/master/coverage.svg)
![Python Version](https://img.shields.io/badge/Python-3.10%2B-blue)
[![PyPI](https://img.shields.io/pypi/v/piscola?label=PyPI&logo=pypi&logoColor=white)](https://pypi.org/project/piscola/)
[![ADS -  2022MNRAS.512.3266M ](https://img.shields.io/badge/ADS-_2022MNRAS.512.3266M_-2ea44f)](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2022MNRAS.512.3266M/abstract)

Read the full documentation at: [piscola.readthedocs.io](http://piscola.readthedocs.io/). See below for a summary. Check [https://github.com/temuller/piscola_templates](https://github.com/temuller/piscola_templates) for spectral templates of different types of supernovae.

___
## Installation

PISCOLA can be installed in the usual way, via pip:

```
pip install piscola
```

or from source:

```
git clone https://github.com/temuller/piscola.git
cd piscola
pip install .
```

If you are using a Mac with M1 or M2 chip and you encounter `RuntimeError: This version of jaxlib was built using AVX instructions, which your CPU and/or operating system do not support. You may be able work around this issue by building jaxlib from source.`, try installing `jax` from anaconda (as suggested [here](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/68327863/importing-jax-fails-on-mac-with-m1-chip)):

```
pip uninstall jax jaxlib
conda install -c conda-forge jaxlib
conda install -c conda-forge jax
```

### Requirements

The requirements can be found in the `requirements.txt` file.

### Tests

To run the tests, go to the parent directory and run the following command:

```
pytest -v
```

## Using PISCOLA

PISCOLA can fit the supernova light curves and correct them in a few lines of code:


```python
sn = piscola.call_sn(<sn_file>)
sn.fit()
```

The light-curve parameters are saved in a dictionary and can be accessed directly:

```python
sn.lc_parameters  # dictionary
```

You can find an example of input file in the [data](https://github.com/temuller/piscola/tree/master/data) directory.

## Citing PISCOLA

If you make use of PISCOLA in your projects, please cite [Müller-Bravo et al. (2022)](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2022MNRAS.512.3266M/abstract). See below for the bibtex format:

```code
@ARTICLE{2022MNRAS.512.3266M,
       author = {{M{\"u}ller-Bravo}, Tom{\'a}s E. and {Sullivan}, Mark and {Smith}, Mathew and {Frohmaier}, Chris and {Guti{\'e}rrez}, Claudia P. and {Wiseman}, Philip and {Zontou}, Zoe},
        title = "{PISCOLA: a data-driven transient light-curve fitter}",
      journal = {\mnras},
     keywords = {supernovae: general, cosmology: observations, distance scale, Astrophysics - High Energy Astrophysical Phenomena, Astrophysics - Cosmology and Nongalactic Astrophysics, Astrophysics - Instrumentation and Methods for Astrophysics, Astrophysics - Solar and Stellar Astrophysics},
         year = 2022,
        month = may,
       volume = {512},
       number = {3},
        pages = {3266-3283},
          doi = {10.1093/mnras/stab3065},
archivePrefix = {arXiv},
       eprint = {2110.11340},
 primaryClass = {astro-ph.HE},
       adsurl = {https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2022MNRAS.512.3266M},
      adsnote = {Provided by the SAO/NASA Astrophysics Data System}
}
```

## Contributing and raising an issue

The recommended way is to use the [issues](https://github.com/temuller/piscola/issues) page or send a pull request. Otherwise, you can contact me directly.

functions.name: piscola.utils.flux_to_mag
documentation:
Converts fluxes to magnitudes, propagating errors if given.

Note: if there are negative or zero fluxes, these are converted to NaN values.

# Parameters

flux

: Array of fluxes.

flux_err

: Array of flux errors.

zp

: Zero points.

# Returns

mag

: Fluxes converted to magnitudes.

mag_err

: Flux errors converted to errors in magnitudes.

functions.name: piscola.utils.mag_to_flux
documentation:
Converts magnitudes to fluxes, propagating errors if given.

# Parameters

mag

: Array of magnitudes.

mag_err

: Array of magnitude errors.

zp

: Zero points.

# Returns

flux

: Magnitudes converted to fluxes.

flux_err

: Magnitude errors converted to errors in fluxes.

functions.name: piscola.utils.calculate_robust_stats
documentation:
Calculates a robust mean and standard deviation not sensitive to outliers.

# Parameters

values

: Array of values.

sigma

: Number of sigmas used for sigma clipping.

# Returns

robust_mean

: Robust mean.

robust_std

: Robust standard deviation.

robust_mask: bool array

: Mask with valid values as `True`.

functions.name: piscola.utils.change_zp
documentation:
Converts flux units given a new zero-point.

**Note:** this assumes that the new zero-point is in the same magnitude system as the current one.

Parameters
----------
flux : float or array
    Fluxes.
zp : float or array
    Current zero-point for the given fluxes.
new_zp : float or array
    New zero-point to convert the flux units.

Returns
-------
new_flux : float or array
    Fluxes with with a new zero-point.

functions.name: piscola.utils._integrate_filter
documentation:
Calculates the flux density of a spectral energy distribution (SED)
given a filter transmission function.

Mainly used by the {func}`extinction_correction` module.

# Parameters

sed_wave

: SED's wavelength range.

sed_flux

: SED's flux density distribution.

filter_wave

: Filter's wavelength range.

filter_transmission

: Filter's transmission function.

response_type

: Filter's response type. Either `photon` or `energy`.

# Returns

flux_filter

: Flux density.

classes.name: piscola.lightcurves_class.Lightcurve

methods.name: piscola.lightcurves_class.Lightcurve.__init__
documentation:
Parameters
----------
band: str
    Light-curve filter/band name.
lcs_df: Dataframe
    Light-curve data: `times`, `fluxes`, `flux_errors`, `magnitudes`, `mag_errors`
    and `magnitude system`.

methods.name: piscola.lightcurves_class.Lightcurve.mask_lc
documentation:
Masks the light-curve data with the given mask.

# Parameters

mask: bool list

: Mask to apply to the light curve.

mask: bool, default `False`

: If `True`, the masked light curves are saved on
  separate arrays.

methods.name: piscola.lightcurves_class.Lightcurve.get_max
documentation:
Calculates the peak magnitude ($m_{max}$) and
its epoch ($t_{max}$).

methods.name: piscola.lightcurves_class.Lightcurve.get_dm15
documentation:
Calculates the classic parameter $\Delta m_{15}$
(Phillips 1993), but using the epoch of peak magnitude of
the current light curve as reference (not necessarily $B$
band).
Light curve class.

classes.name: piscola.lightcurves_class.Lightcurves

methods.name: piscola.lightcurves_class.Lightcurves.get_max_colour
documentation:
Calculates the colour at the epoch of peak magnitude
in `band1`.

The colour is $(band1 - band2)$ at the time of
peak magnitude in `band1`.

# Parameters

band1: str

: Name of first band.

band2: str

: Name of second band.

# Returns

colour: float

: Colour at the epoch of peak magnitude in `band1`.

colour_err: float

: Uncertainty on the colour parameter.

methods.name: piscola.lightcurves_class.Lightcurves.get_colour_stretch
documentation:
Calculates the colour stretch parameter (Burns et al. 2014).

The formula used is $(T(band1-band2)_{max} - T_{max}/30)$, where
$T(band1−band2)_{max}$ is the time of maximum (reddest colour)
in the $(band1−band2)$ colour curve and $T_{max}$ is the
epoch of peak magnitude in `band1`.

# Parameters

band1: str

: Name of first band.

band2: str

: Name of second band.

# Returns

stretch: float

: Colour-stretch parameter.

stretch_err: float

: Uncertainty on the colour-stretch parameter.

methods.name: piscola.lightcurves_class.Lightcurves.get_lc_params
documentation:
Calculates the peak magnitude ($m_{max}$), its epoch
($t_{max}$) and $\Delta m_{15}$ for all bands.
Multi-colour light curves class.

modules.name: piscola._version
documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

functions.name: piscola.gaussian_process.prepare_gp_inputs
documentation:
Prepares the inputs for the Gaussian Process model fitting.

# Parameters

times: ndarray

: Light-curve epochs.

wavelengths: ndarray

: Light-curve effective wavelength.

fluxes: ndarray

: Light-curve fluxes.

flux_errors: ndarray

: Light-curve flux errors.

fit_type: str

: Transformation used for the light-curve fits: `flux`, `log`, `arcsinh`.

wave_log: bool

: Whether to use logarithmic (base 10) scale for the
  wavelength axis.

# Returns

X: ndarray

: X-axis array for the Gaussian Process model.

y: ndarray

: Y-axis array for the Gaussian Process model.

yerr: ndarray

: Y-axis errors for the Gaussian Process model.

y_norm: float

: Normalisation used for the fluxes and errors. The maximum
  of the fluxes is used.

functions.name: piscola.gaussian_process.fit_gp_model
documentation:
Fits a Gaussian Process model to a SN multi-colour light curve.

All input arrays MUST have the same length.

# Parameters

times: ndarray

: Light-curve epochs.

wavelengths: ndarray

: Light-curve effective wavelength.

fluxes: ndarray

: Light-curve fluxes.

flux_errors: ndarray

: Light-curve flux errors.

k1: str

: Kernel to be used for the time axis. Either `Matern52`,
  `Matern32` or `ExpSquared`.

fit_type: str

: Transformation used for the light-curve fits: `flux`, `log`, `arcsinh`.

wave_log: bool, default `True`.

: Whether to use logarithmic (base 10) scale for the
  wavelength axis.

time_scale: float, default `None`

: If given, the time scale is fixed using this value, in units of days.

wave_scale: float, default `None`

: If given, the wavelength scale is fixed using this value, in units of angstroms.
  Note that if 'wave_log=True', the logarithm base 10 of this value is used.

add_noise: bool, default `True`

: Whether to add a white-noise component to the GP model. This "inflates" the errors.

# Returns

gp_model: ~tinygp.gp.GaussianProcess

: Gaussian Process multi-colour light-curve model.

functions.name: piscola.gaussian_process.fit_single_lightcurve
documentation:
Fits a Gaussian Process model to a single SN light curve.

All input arrays MUST have the same length.

# Parameters

times: ndarray

: Light-curve epochs.

fluxes: ndarray

: Light-curve fluxes.

flux_errors: ndarray

: Light-curve flux errors.

k1: str

: Kernel to be used for the time axis. Either `Matern52`,
  `Matern32` or `ExpSquared`.

# Returns

gp_model: ~tinygp.gp.GaussianProcess

: Gaussian Process light-curve model.

functions.name: piscola.filters_class.trim_filters
documentation:
Trim the leading and trailing zeros from a 1-D array or sequence, leaving
one zero on each side. This is a modified version of {func}`numpy.trim_zeros`.

# Parameters

response

: Input array.

# Returns

first

: Index of the last leading zero.

last

: Index of the first trailing zero.

classes.name: piscola.filters_class.SingleFilter

methods.name: piscola.filters_class.SingleFilter.__init__
documentation:
Parameters
----------
band: str
    Name of the band.
mag_sys: str
    Magnitude system.

methods.name: piscola.filters_class.SingleFilter._add_filter
documentation:
Adds a filter from the available filters
in the PISCOLA library.

Parameters
----------
filt_name: str
    Name of the filter.
mag_sys: str
    Magnitude system.

methods.name: piscola.filters_class.SingleFilter.calc_eff_wave
documentation:
Calculates the effective wavelength.

The effective wavelength depends on the shape
of the given SED.

# Parameters

sed_wave

: SED's wavelength range. If `None`, a flat SED is used.

sed_flux

: SED's flux density distribution. If `None`, a flat SED is used.

# Returns

eff_wave: float

: Effective wavelength.

methods.name: piscola.filters_class.SingleFilter.integrate_filter
documentation:
Calculates the flux density of an SED given a filter response.

Parameters
----------
sed_wave : array-like
    SED's wavelength range.
sed_flux : array-like
    SED's flux density distribution.

Returns
-------
flux_filter : float
    Flux density.

methods.name: piscola.filters_class.SingleFilter._get_standard_flux
documentation:
Calculates the integrated flux of a standard star SED.

Parameters
----------
mag_sys_file: str
    File with the magnitude system information.

Returns
-------
f_sed: float
    Integrated flux density.

methods.name: piscola.filters_class.SingleFilter._get_standard_mag
documentation:
Obtains the tabulated magnitude of a standard star.

Parameters
----------
mag_sys_file: str
    File with the magnitude system information.

Returns
-------
sed_mag: float
    Tabulated standard magnitude.

methods.name: piscola.filters_class.SingleFilter.calculate_zp
documentation:
Calculates the zero point in the `AB`, `Vega` or `BD17`
magnitude systems.

# Parameters

mag_sys

: Magnitude system. For example: `AB`, `BD17` or `Vega`.

# Returns

zp

: Zero-point in the given natural magnitude system.

methods.name: piscola.filters_class.SingleFilter.calculate_extinction
documentation:
Calculates the extinction for a given filter, given a right ascension and declination or $E(B-V)$.

# Parameters

ra

: Right ascension in degrees.

dec

: Declination in degrees.

scaling: float, default `0.86`

: Calibration of the Milky Way dust maps. Either `0.86`
  for the Schlafly & Finkbeiner (2011) recalibration or `1.0` for the original
  dust map of Schlegel, Fikbeiner & Davis (1998).

reddening_law: str, default `fitzpatrick99`

: Reddening law. The options are: `ccm89` (Cardelli, Clayton & Mathis 1989), `odonnell94` (O’Donnell 1994),
  `fitzpatrick99` (Fitzpatrick 1999), `calzetti00` (Calzetti 2000) and `fm07` (Fitzpatrick & Massa 2007 with
  $R_V = 3.1$.)

r_v

: Total-to-selective extinction ratio ($R_V$)

ebv

: Colour excess ($E(B-V)$). If given, this is used instead of the dust map value.

# Returns

A

: Extinction value in magnitudes.
Single filter class.

classes.name: piscola.filters_class.MultiFilters

methods.name: piscola.filters_class.MultiFilters.__init__
documentation:
# Parameters

bands: list-like

: Bands to include. If `None`, only include
  Bessell filters.

mag_systems: list-like

: Magnitude systems. E.g. AB, Vega.

methods.name: piscola.filters_class.MultiFilters._add_filter
documentation:
Adds a band object with a single-filter class.

Parameters
----------
band: str
    Name of the band.

methods.name: piscola.filters_class.MultiFilters.remove_filter
documentation:
Removes a band object.

Parameters
----------
band: str
    Name of the band.

methods.name: piscola.filters_class.MultiFilters.calc_eff_wave
documentation:
Calculates the effective wavelength of multiple bands.

The effective wavelength depends on the shape
of the given SED.

# Parameters

bands: list-like, default `None`

: Bands for calculating their effective wavelengths. If `None`,
  use of the available bands.

sed_wave

: SED's wavelength range. If `None`, a flat SED is used.

sed_flux

: SED's flux density distribution. If `None`, a flat SED is used.

methods.name: piscola.filters_class.MultiFilters.calc_pivot
documentation:
Calculates the observed band closest to restframe
$B$ band ($4500 Å$).

# Parameters

z

: Redshift.

methods.name: piscola.filters_class.MultiFilters.plot_filters
documentation:
Plot the filters' transmission functions.

# Parameters

bands

: Bands to plot. If `None`, plots all the available bands.
Class representing multiple filters.

functions.name: piscola.extinction_correction._download_dustmaps
documentation:
Downloads the dust maps for extinction calculation if they are not found
locally.

functions.name: piscola.extinction_correction.redden
documentation:
Reddens the given spectrum, given a right ascension and declination or $E(B-V)$.

# Parameters

wave

: Wavelength values.

flux

: Flux density values.

ra

: Right ascension in degrees.

dec

: Declination in degrees.

scaling: float, default `0.86`

: Calibration of the Milky Way dust maps. Either `0.86`
  for the Schlafly & Finkbeiner (2011) recalibration or `1.0` for the original
  dust map of Schlegel, Fikbeiner & Davis (1998).

reddening_law: str, default `fitzpatrick99`

: Reddening law. The options are: `ccm89` (Cardelli, Clayton & Mathis 1989), `odonnell94` (O’Donnell 1994),
  `fitzpatrick99` (Fitzpatrick 1999), `calzetti00` (Calzetti 2000) and `fm07` (Fitzpatrick & Massa 2007 with
  $R_V = 3.1$.)

r_v

: Total-to-selective extinction ratio ($R_V$)

ebv

: Colour excess ($E(B-V)$). If given, this is used instead of the dust map value.

# Returns

redden_flux

: Redden flux values.

functions.name: piscola.extinction_correction.deredden
documentation:
Dereddens the given spectrum, given a right ascension and declination or $E(B-V)$.

# Parameters

wave

: Wavelength values.

flux

: Flux density values.

ra

: Right ascension in degrees.

dec

: Declination in degrees.

scaling: float, default `0.86`

: Calibration of the Milky Way dust maps. Either `0.86`
  for the Schlafly & Finkbeiner (2011) recalibration or `1.0` for the original
  dust map of Schlegel, Fikbeiner & Davis (1998).

reddening_law: str, default `fitzpatrick99`

: Reddening law. The options are: `ccm89` (Cardelli, Clayton & Mathis 1989), `odonnell94` (O’Donnell 1994),
  `fitzpatrick99` (Fitzpatrick 1999), `calzetti00` (Calzetti 2000) and `fm07` (Fitzpatrick & Massa 2007 with
  $R_V = 3.1$.)

r_v

: Total-to-selective extinction ratio ($R_V$)

ebv

: Colour excess ($E(B-V)$). If given, this is used instead of the dust map value.

# Returns

deredden_flux

: Deredden flux values.

functions.name: piscola.extinction_correction.calculate_ebv
documentation:
Calculates Milky Way colour excess, $E(B-V)$, for a given right ascension and declination.

# Parameters

ra

: Right ascension in degrees.

dec

: Declination in degrees.

scaling: float, default `0.86`

: Calibration of the Milky Way dust maps. Either `0.86`
  for the Schlafly & Finkbeiner (2011) recalibration or `1.0` for the original
  dust map of Schlegel, Finkbeiner & Davis (1998).

# Returns

ebv

: Reddening value, $E(B-V)`$.

functions.name: piscola.extinction_correction.extinction_filter
documentation:
Calculates the extinction for a given filter, given a right ascension and declination or $E(B-V)$.

# Parameters

filter_wave

: Filter's wavelength range.

filter_response

: Filter's response function.

ra

: Right ascension in degrees.

dec

: Declination in degrees.

scaling: float, default `0.86`

: Calibration of the Milky Way dust maps. Either `0.86`
  for the Schlafly & Finkbeiner (2011) recalibration or `1.0` for the original
  dust map of Schlegel, Fikbeiner & Davis (1998).

reddening_law: str, default `fitzpatrick99`

: Reddening law. The options are: `ccm89` (Cardelli, Clayton & Mathis 1989), `odonnell94` (O’Donnell 1994),
  `fitzpatrick99` (Fitzpatrick 1999), `calzetti00` (Calzetti 2000) and `fm07` (Fitzpatrick & Massa 2007 with
  $R_V = 3.1$.)

r_v

: Total-to-selective extinction ratio ($R_V$)

ebv

: Colour excess ($E(B-V)$). If given, this is used instead of the dust map value.

# Returns

A

: Extinction value in magnitudes.

functions.name: piscola.extinction_correction.extinction_at_wavelength
documentation:
Calculates the extinction at a given wavelength, given a right ascension and declination or $E(B-V)$.

# Parameters

: Wavelength in agnstroms.

ra

: Right ascension in degrees.

dec

: Declination in degrees.

reddening_law: str, default `fitzpatrick99`

: Reddening law. The options are: `ccm89` (Cardelli, Clayton & Mathis 1989), `odonnell94` (O’Donnell 1994),
  `fitzpatrick99` (Fitzpatrick 1999), `calzetti00` (Calzetti 2000) and `fm07` (Fitzpatrick & Massa 2007 with
  $R_V = 3.1$.)

r_v

: Total-to-selective extinction ratio ($R_V$)

ebv

: Colour excess ($E(B-V)$). If given, this is used instead of the dust map value.

# Returns

A

: Extinction value in magnitudes.

functions.name: piscola.extinction_correction.extinction_curve
documentation:
Plots the extinction curve for a given right ascension and declination or $E(B-V)$.

# Parameters

ra

: Right ascension in degrees.

dec

: Declination in degrees.

scaling: float, default `0.86`

: Calibration of the Milky Way dust maps. Either `0.86`
  for the Schlafly & Finkbeiner (2011) recalibration or `1.0` for the original
  dust map of Schlegel, Fikbeiner & Davis (1998).

reddening_law: str, default `fitzpatrick99`

: Reddening law. The options are: `ccm89` (Cardelli, Clayton & Mathis 1989), `odonnell94` (O’Donnell 1994),
  `fitzpatrick99` (Fitzpatrick 1999), `calzetti00` (Calzetti 2000) and `fm07` (Fitzpatrick & Massa 2007 with
  $R_V = 3.1$.)

r_v

: Total-to-selective extinction ratio ($R_V$)

ebv

: Colour excess ($E(B-V)$). If given, this is used instead of the dust map value.

modules.name: piscola.sn_class
documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
This is the skeleton of PISCOLA, the main file

functions.name: piscola.sn_class.call_sn
documentation:
Loads a supernova from a file and initialises
the {func}`sn` object. The object is initialised
with all the necessary information like filters,
fluxes, etc.

# Parameters

lc_file

: File with the SN info and light curves.

# Returns

sn_obj

: New {func}`sn` object.

functions.name: piscola.sn_class.load_sn
documentation:
Loads a {func}`sn` oject that was previously saved as a pickle file.

# Parameters

piscola_file

: File with the SN object saved with PISCOLA.

# Returns

pickle.load(file)

: {func}`sn` object previously saved as a pickle file.

classes.name: piscola.sn_class.Supernova

methods.name: piscola.sn_class.Supernova.__init__
documentation:
# Parameters

name: str

: Name of the supernova.

z: float, default `0.0`

: Redshift of the supernova.

ra: float, default `None`

: Right ascension of the supernova.

dec: float, default `None`

: Declination of the supernova.

lc_file: str, default `None`

: File with the supernova light-curve data.

methods.name: piscola.sn_class.Supernova.add_filter
documentation:
Adds a new filter.

Parameters
----------
band : str
    Name of the filter
mag_sys : str
    Magnitude system.

methods.name: piscola.sn_class.Supernova.save
documentation:
Saves a SN object into a pickle file

# Parameters

path: str, default `None`

: Path where to save the SN object. If `None`,
  use current directory. The file will be saved with
  the name `<SN name>.pisco`.

methods.name: piscola.sn_class.Supernova._normalize_lcs
documentation:
Normalizes the fluxes and zero-points (ZPs).

Fluxes are converted to physical units by calculating the ZPs according to the
magnitude system, for example: **AB**, **BD17** or **Vega**.

methods.name: piscola.sn_class.Supernova._stack_lcs
documentation:
Stacks of light-curve properties

Times, wavelengths, fluxes, magnitudes and errors are
stacked for 2D fitting.

Parameters
----------
bands: list-like
    Bands used for the stacking.
Light-curve fits #

methods.name: piscola.sn_class.Supernova.gp_predict
documentation:
Returns the Gaussian Process prediction of the light-curve fits.

Parameters
----------
times_pred : ndarray
    Time-axis grid.
wavelengths_pred : ndarray
    Wavelength-axis grid.
return_cov : bool, optional
    Whether to return the covariance or variance.

Returns
-------
mu: ndarray
    Mean of the Gaussian Process.
cov: ndarray
    Either the variance or covariance of the Gaussian Process.

methods.name: piscola.sn_class.Supernova.fit
documentation:
Fits the observed multi-colour light-curve data with Gaussian Process.

The time of rest-frame B-band peak luminosity is estimated by finding where the derivative is equal to zero.

# Parameters

bands

: Bands used for fitting light curves. By default, use all the available bands.

k1: str

: Kernel to be used for the time axis. Either `Matern52`,
  `Matern32` or `ExpSquared`.

fit_type: str, default `flux`.

: Transformation used for the light-curve fits: `flux`, `log`, `arcsinh`.

wave_log: bool, default `True`.

: Whether to use logarithmic (base 10) scale for the
  wavelength axis.

time_scale: float, default `None`

: If given, the time scale is fixed using this value, in units of days.

wave_scale: float, default `None`

: If given, the wavelength scale is fixed using this value, in units of angstroms.
  Note that if 'wave_log=True', the logarithm base 10 of this value is used.

add_noise: bool, default `True`

: Whether to add a white-noise component to the GP model. This "inflates" the errors.

methods.name: piscola.sn_class.Supernova._get_rest_lightcurves
documentation:
Obtains the corrected rest-frame light curves from the fits.

# Returns

bands

: Bands for which to extract the light curves. All bands are used by default.

methods.name: piscola.sn_class.Supernova._calculate_lc_params
documentation: Calculate rest-frame light-curve parameters.

methods.name: piscola.sn_class.Supernova._calculate_lc_params2
documentation: Calculate light-curve parameters with the time in restframe.

methods.name: piscola.sn_class.Supernova.get_obs_params
documentation:
Obtains the time and magnitude of maximum for the observed light curves.

No corrections are applied at all.

# Returns

bands

: Bands for which to extract the parameters.
Extra Functions #
this are not essential but help assessing the fits
and output results, among other things

methods.name: piscola.sn_class.Supernova.plot_lcs
documentation:
Plots the observed light-curves.

# Parameters

plot_mag

: If `True`, plots the bands in magnitude space.

fig_name

: If given, name of the output plot.

methods.name: piscola.sn_class.Supernova.plot_fits
documentation:
Plots the light-curves fits results.

Plots the observed data for each band together with the gaussian process fits.
The time of $B$-band peak is shown as a vertical dashed line.

# Parameters

plot_mag

: If `True`, plots the bands in magnitude space.

fig_name

: If given, name of the output plot.

methods.name: piscola.sn_class.Supernova.plot_rest_fits
documentation:
Plots the rest-frame light-curves fits results.

Plots the observed data for each band together with the gaussian process fits.
The time of $B$-band peak is shown as a vertical dashed line.

# Parameters

plot_mag

: If `True`, plots the bands in magnitude space.

fig_name

: If given, name of the output plot.

methods.name: piscola.sn_class.Supernova.export_fits
documentation:
Exports the light-curve fits into an output file.

# Parameters

output_file

: Name of the output file. If `None`, the output
  file is of the form `<SN name>_fits.dat`.

methods.name: piscola.sn_class.Supernova.export_restframe_lcs
documentation:
Exports the corrected, rest-frame light-curves into an output file.

# Parameters

output_file

: Name of the output file. If `None`, the output

file is of the form `<SN name>_restframe_lcs.dat`.
Class representing a supernova object.
